Now, if you look at this from a high level, my laptop is made up of components like hardware. and software and there is an operating system. The idea is you trust nothing and verify everything regardless of whether that transaction or request is coming in. Like when you look at traditionally, and historically how we've built architectures anything inside of your network perimeter for example you know has this implied level of trust already because it's inside of your network this concept of zero trust removes that So the idea is it doesn't matter where anything originates, whether it's inside of your network or outside of your network, you start with no trust at all, which means that if you're going to allow certain things, It requires a higher level of authentication and verification and real-time monitoring and device authentication and everything else. So we start with trusting nothing. In other words, more logging, more real-time monitoring, for example. And a perfect example of where this comes in, shared responsibilities, is again, cloud, right? So we need to remember what we talked about in domain number one. And that is the difference between accountability and responsibility. Right.
